draft.introduction: The Han Feizi is the foundational text of Chinese Legalism, arguing that human nature is self-interested and that good governance requires clear laws, impartial punishment, and institutional mechanisms rather than moral cultivation.

translation.text: The Confucians with their learning disturb the laws; the knights-errant with their martial prowess violate the prohibitions. In times of peace the ruler honors the Confucians; in times of crisis he employs warriors. Those he honors are not those he uses; those he uses are not those he honors. This is why the state falls into disorder.

translator_notes.note: Han Fei’s critique of Confucian moralism anticipates Machiavelli by seventeen centuries: effective governance requires institutions, not virtuous individuals. The “five vermin” (scholars, speech-makers, swordsmen, draft-dodgers, merchants) represent groups whose private interests undermine state power.
